title: Bullet Energy Calculator – Muzzle Energy & Momentum
tagline: Calculate the kinetic energy (in foot-pounds) and momentum of any projectile from its weight and velocity in seconds.
intro: Enter the bullet weight in grains and muzzle velocity in feet per second to get kinetic energy and momentum instantly.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for both fields.

about.heading: About the Bullet Energy Calculator
about.body:
The bullet energy calculator computes two critical ballistic quantities — kinetic energy and momentum — from two straightforward inputs: bullet weight in grains and muzzle velocity in feet per second. These measurements are the universal language of external ballistics and are printed on nearly every box of centerfire ammunition sold in the United States.

Kinetic energy is a measure of the work a moving projectile can perform upon impact. The formula comes directly from classical mechanics: KE = ½mv². Because bullet weights are traditionally measured in grains (where 7,000 grains equal one pound avoirdupois), the denominator becomes a fixed conversion constant: KE (ft-lb) = (mass_grains × velocity_fps²) / 450,436. The constant 450,436 folds together the grain-to-pound conversion (7,000) and the factor of 2 from the ½ in the kinetic-energy formula, combined with the unit for foot-pounds. This formula is the industry standard used by ballisticians, ammunition manufacturers, and hunting regulations worldwide.

Momentum (in lb·ft/s) measures the quantity of motion — it predicts how well a projectile maintains its velocity in crosswinds and at long range. Momentum = (mass_grains × velocity_fps) / 225,218. The denominator is half of 450,436 because momentum uses v¹ rather than v². Although energy determines terminal performance at the target, momentum better describes the felt recoil impulse transferred back to the shooter via Newton's third law.

Why does bullet energy matter? Hunting regulations in many jurisdictions specify minimum muzzle-energy thresholds for ethical harvest of specific game species — 1,000 ft-lb is a common floor for deer-sized animals, and 2,500 ft-lb or more is often required for elk or moose. Self-defense ballistics researchers track energy as one factor — alongside expansion, penetration depth, and projectile construction — when evaluating terminal effectiveness. Competitive shooters use power factor (momentum-derived) as a division classifier in USPSA, IPSC, and similar sports.

A few practical notes: velocity drops faster than weight affects energy — doubling speed quadruples energy while doubling weight only doubles it. This is why high-velocity loads from smaller calibers often exceed the energy of slower loads from larger calibers. Muzzle energy is also a snapshot at the barrel crown; downrange energy falls continuously as drag decelerates the bullet. For long-range hunting or suppressed-fire analysis, pair this calculator with a ballistic coefficient (BC) solver for a complete picture.

examples.heading: Bullet Energy Examples
examples.intro: Representative calculations for common cartridges across different shooting disciplines.

items.note: Classic deer-hunting load; comfortably exceeds the 1,000 ft-lb minimum for whitetail at typical hunting ranges.

Light rimfire cartridge suited for small game and plinking; minimal recoil due to low momentum.

howto.heading: How to Use the Bullet Energy Calculator
steps: Enter the bullet weight in grains into the 'Bullet Weight' field. This value is printed on the ammunition box or cartridge data sheet.
Enter the muzzle velocity in feet per second (fps). Use manufacturer-published data or a chronograph reading.
Click 'Calculate' to instantly see the kinetic energy in foot-pounds and momentum in lb·ft/s.
Compare the kinetic energy result against the minimum energy threshold required by hunting regulations or self-defense standards for your intended application.
Click 'Reset' to clear the fields and start a fresh calculation for a different load.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: What is the formula for bullet kinetic energy?
faq.q1_a: Bullet kinetic energy in foot-pounds equals (bullet weight in grains × muzzle velocity in fps squared) divided by 450,436. The constant 450,436 converts grains to pounds and accounts for the factor of 2 in the ½mv² formula, yielding results directly in foot-pounds.
faq.q2: How many grains are in a pound?
faq.q2_a: There are exactly 7,000 grains in one avoirdupois pound. Bullet weights are almost always stated in grains because the grain is fine-grained enough to meaningfully distinguish projectiles — a difference of 5 grains (less than 0.03 oz) can measurably change ballistic performance.
faq.q3: What is the difference between energy and momentum in ballistics?
faq.q3_a: Energy (½mv²) measures the capacity to do work at impact and scales with the square of velocity, while momentum (mv) measures the quantity of motion and scales linearly with velocity. Energy predicts wound cavity potential in soft tissue, while momentum is used to calculate recoil impulse and is the basis for power-factor divisions in competitive shooting.
faq.q4: How much bullet energy do I need for deer hunting?
faq.q4_a: Most state wildlife agencies and hunting organisations recommend a minimum of 1,000 ft-lb at the point of impact for ethical harvest of deer-sized game. For larger animals such as elk or moose, a common guideline is 1,500 – 2,500 ft-lb. Always check the specific regulations for your jurisdiction and intended quarry.
faq.q5: Why does velocity matter more than bullet weight for energy?
faq.q5_a: Because kinetic energy is proportional to velocity squared (v²) but only linearly proportional to mass, a 10% increase in velocity raises energy by about 21% while a 10% increase in bullet weight raises energy by only 10%. This is why magnum cartridges achieve their energy advantage primarily through higher velocity rather than heavier projectiles.
faq.q6: Can I use this calculator for arrows or other projectiles?
faq.q6_a: Yes. The formula E = (mass_grains × velocity_fps²) / 450,436 applies to any projectile whose weight is measured in grains and velocity in fps. Archers commonly use it to calculate arrow kinetic energy; typical hunting arrows produce 40–80 ft-lb, which is sufficient for whitetail deer at short range.
